title: A goal is something you reach. A value is a direction you walk.
body: Goals end — you hit them or you don't. Values don't end. "Run a marathon" is a goal. "Be someone who looks after their body" is a value. The marathon is a way of expressing the value, but the value continues after the medal.
reflectionQuestion: What's a recent goal — and what's the value underneath it?

title: If someone described you at your funeral, what would you want them to say?
body: The eulogy exercise cuts through everything else. The person you most want to be described as — that's your real values list. Anything you're spending time on that wouldn't make it into the eulogy is asking a hard question.
reflectionQuestion: Which sentence in your imagined eulogy is closest to true today, and which is farthest?

title: A value you'll only defend when it costs nothing isn't really yours.
body: Honesty when no-one will mind. Kindness toward people you like. Integrity when it pays. Values reveal themselves under cost. The ones you hold when it costs you are your actual values.
reflectionQuestion: When did a value of yours cost you something recently — and did you hold it?

title: The things you pretend not to want are the loudest signal.
body: Whatever you've talked yourself out of wanting — the career change, the creative work, the move, the relationship — usually contains real information. Not always a directive. But information.
reflectionQuestion: What have you been telling yourself you don't really want?

title: External rewards crowd out internal ones.
body: Self-determination research shows pay, praise, prizes can actually reduce motivation for things you used to do for their own sake. Notice which of your activities are still intrinsically rewarding — and protect them from being optimised.
reflectionQuestion: What activity of yours is shifting from "I love this" to "this is paying off"?

title: Most people are underusing the strengths they already have.
body: Character-strengths research suggests the move with most impact is not fixing your weaknesses but deploying your strengths in places they haven't been used yet. Curiosity, kindness, courage, fairness, persistence — find a new room to use yours in.
reflectionQuestion: What's a strength you take for granted that you could deliberately use somewhere new this week?

title: Ask the 80-year-old version of you, not the anxious 30-year-old.
body: Bezos popularised it, but the move is ancient: when stuck on a decision, ask which choice your future self will regret less. The 80-year-old usually has a clearer view than the anxious now-you.
reflectionQuestion: What decision would the 80-year-old version of you make differently than you're currently leaning?

title: Look at your calendar to see your real values.
body: Stated values are aspirational. Your time and money are evidential. If you say family matters most and you give them an hour a day, look at the gap honestly. The gap is the real conversation.
reflectionQuestion: Where is the biggest gap between what you say you value and where your time actually goes?

title: Meaning is constructed, not discovered.
body: There's no hidden meaning waiting for you to find it. Meaning is made by what you commit to, what you protect, what you give yourself to. The good news: you don't have to find it. The hard news: you have to make it.
reflectionQuestion: What are you currently committed to that gives meaning, and what are you avoiding committing to?

title: If you had one sentence — what would you orient by?
body: "I want to be someone who…" One sentence. Three or four values, max. Specific enough to be useful in decisions. Vague enough to last decades. Most people have never written theirs down.
reflectionQuestion: Write your one sentence today. Edit it next week. Edit it again.

title: Your values are not negotiable with the algorithm.
body: The feed will tell you what to want. The advertising will tell you what to want. The peer group will tell you what to want. Knowing your values means knowing the difference between what you actually want and what the world is offering you.
reflectionQuestion: What have you been pulled toward this month that doesn't actually fit who you want to be?

title: Your values will change. That's not betrayal.
body: A 25-year-old who valued adventure and freedom may, at 45, value continuity and depth. That's not selling out — it's life-stage. The work is to stay honest about what you currently value, not to stay loyal to a past version.
reflectionQuestion: What's a value you've outgrown — and what's the new one underneath it?
